locked_facts: KE + PE = constant, when only conservative forces do work
Gravitational PE: U_gravity = mgh (relative to chosen reference)
Elastic PE of spring: U_spring = ½kx²
At highest point of projectile: KE is minimum, PE is maximum
Non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) dissipate mechanical energy as heat
Total energy (including heat) is always conserved — first law of thermodynamics
truth_statements: The total mechanical energy of a system is conserved if only conservative forces act on it
Conservative forces are path-independent — work depends only on initial and final positions
Gravitational force, spring force, and electrostatic force are conservative; friction and air drag are not
Energy can transform between kinetic and potential forms, but the sum remains constant in conservative systems
When non-conservative forces act, mechanical energy decreases by the amount of work done against them

aha_moment.what_to_show: A ball rolling down a frictionless curved track — KE bar and PE bar change continuously but their SUM bar stays perfectly flat. At the bottom: KE is maximum, PE is zero. At the top: PE is maximum, KE is zero.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: KE + PE = E_total line stays horizontal while KE and PE bars seesaw. Numerical values update in real time.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see energy is NOT lost — it transforms. The total energy bar never changes height.

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A ball at the top of a curved frictionless track. Height h marked. PE bar is full, KE bar is zero. Total energy E = mgh shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: All energy is potential — ball is at rest at height h

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Ball released and rolls down the track. PE bar shrinks, KE bar grows. At midpoint: PE = KE = E_total/2. Speed increases as height decreases.
simulation_states.emphasis: PE converts to KE as ball descends — energy is transforming, not being created

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Ball at the bottom: KE = mgh, PE = 0. Ball rises on other side back to same height. KE + PE bar stays flat throughout. Numerical proof: E_total at top = E_total at bottom.
simulation_states.emphasis: Total energy bar is CONSTANT — energy transforms but is never lost in the absence of friction

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: add friction (non-conservative force) and see total energy decrease. Toggle spring at bottom to see elastic PE. Adjust initial height and mass.
simulation_states.emphasis: With friction ON, the total energy bar shrinks each cycle. Without friction, ball returns to exact same height.

common_misconceptions.belief: Energy is used up or consumed when an object moves
common_misconceptions.correction: Energy is NEVER destroyed (first law of thermodynamics). In a conservative system, KE + PE stays constant. Even with friction, energy converts to heat — total energy of the universe is conserved.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show KE + PE bar staying flat on frictionless track. With friction, show heat bar growing while mechanical energy bar shrinks — total stays constant.

common_misconceptions.belief: A ball thrown up loses energy as it rises
common_misconceptions.correction: The ball's KE decreases but PE increases by exactly the same amount. Total mechanical energy is unchanged. At the top, all KE has converted to PE, not disappeared.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show ball going up with KE bar shrinking and PE bar growing by equal amount — total flat

legend.meaning: Total mechanical energy E = KE + PE
Energy dissipated by friction (when friction is on)
